---
title: "How Information is Measured?"
date: 2026-04-14
description: "From surprise to entropy — the mathematical foundations of measuring information"
categories: [entropy, foundations, shannon]
---
> "Information is a difference that makes a difference."
> — Gregory Bateson

The etymology of the word information comes from _informare_ in latin, meaning - to give form. Let's ponder on how can we measure information using the analogy to energy. Energy is an ancient concept that came from [[Aristotle]], who coined the term Ergon, coming from the Proto-Indo-European root _*werg_ that means "to do", in the sense of to do work. So the important thing about energy is that we can measure it, and that you can measure the amount of energy in heat, or work (the physical concept of work), and many other forms of energy. The [[Bit]] is the unit of information, and it stands for - BInary digiT. The idea is that a bit is a measure of difference, between two possibilities: 0 and 1, True or False, Hot or Cold, etc.

## Bateson's Definition

Gregory Bateson was a polymath — anthropologist, cyberneticist, biologist, and systems theorist — whose work spanned the mid-20th century. His definition of information as *"a difference that makes a difference"* comes from his 1972 collection *Steps to an Ecology of Mind*, and it has two distinct parts worth unpacking carefully.

**The first "difference"** is physical — a distinction in the world. The contrast between light and shadow on a page. A change in air pressure that reaches your eardrum. A particular sequence of base pairs in DNA rather than another.

**The second "difference"** is what happens *to a receiver*. The same physical difference can be information to one system and noise to another. A bat hears the difference between echoes that you cannot. Your immune system "reads" a molecular difference that your conscious mind is entirely unaware of.

So Bateson's definition quietly smuggles in something radical: **information is not a property of a signal alone, but of a relationship between a signal and a receiver**. A difference only *becomes* information when it lands somewhere that can be changed by it — which brings us back to the etymology: *informare*, to give form. Information is whatever reshapes the receiver's internal form.

## How Much Can *n* Bits Hold?

The relationship between bits and the number of things they can represent is exponential: *n* bits can distinguish exactly **2ⁿ distinct states**. One bit gives you 2 possibilities. Ten bits give you 1,024 — just over a thousand. Thirty bits give you over a billion. The number grows so fast that a relatively small number of bits can represent an almost incomprehensibly large number of things.

A useful approximation to keep in mind: **2¹⁰ ≈ 10³**, meaning every 10 bits you add multiplies your capacity by roughly a thousand. So 20 bits ≈ one million states, 30 bits ≈ one billion, and so on.

## Labeling Everything

One of the most striking applications of this is labeling. You don't need to *understand* something to give it a unique name — you just need enough bits to distinguish it from everything else. The supermarket barcode works exactly this way: a short sequence of binary stripes, encoding a number that points to a specific product in a database.

Scaling this up: the observable universe contains approximately 10⁹⁰ elementary particles. Since 2¹⁰ ≈ 10³, we have:

$$2^{300} \approx 10^{90}$$

So **300 bits suffice to assign a unique label to every particle in the observable universe**. You could, in principle, write a barcode for every photon, every electron, every quark — and that barcode would be only 300 digits long in binary.

## How Many Bits Does the Universe Contain?

The observable universe — everything within roughly 13 billion light-years, accessible to us since the Big Bang — is not infinite. And remarkably, neither is the information it contains. This follows from the laws of quantum mechanics, which place hard physical limits on how much information any region of space can hold.

The universe contains around $10^{90}$ particles, most of them microwave photons — remnants of the Big Bang that permeate all of space right now, invisible to us but everywhere. Each carries a small number of bits. But if we push to the absolute limits allowed by physics — accounting for every possible degree of freedom at the finest scales — the total information capacity of the observable universe is estimated at:

$$10^{120} \text{ bits}$$

That is a 1 followed by 120 zeros. It is an enormous number, but it is *finite*. You can always name a larger number — $10^{121}$, or $10^{240}$ — which means the universe, for all its vastness, does not contain all possible information. It is a very large message, but a bounded one.

For comparison, human DNA encodes around **six billion bits** — roughly 3 billion base pairs, with 4 possible bases at each position (2 bits per pair). That's vastly more than the 300 bits needed to label every particle in the universe, yet still an almost inconceivably small fraction of the universe's total information capacity. And what does all that genetic information encode? Not labels, but *possibilities* — the genetic combinations that program a living organism. We share 99.8% of those bits with other humans, 99% with chimpanzees, and most of the rest with yeast. What makes you *you* is a remarkably thin sliver of difference. Bateson's insight, made literal.

## Information Can Be Measured Independently of Meaning

This is the crucial insight Claude Shannon gave us: **information can be quantified without asking what it means**. A 300-bit label for a microwave photon carries the same number of bits as a 300-bit passage of poetry — the physical quantity of information is identical, even though one is arbitrary and the other is rich with significance.

This is precisely where Shannon's framework diverges from Bateson's. Shannon measures information at the level of the signal — pure distinguishability, independent of receiver or context. Bateson reminds us that a difference only *becomes* information when it lands on something that can be changed by it. Both perspectives are true, and the tension between them runs through everything that follows.
